project.title: Analysis of the interactome of B2-SINE RNA transcripts
project.projectDescription: B2-SINEs are noncoding RNAs which are transcribed by RNA polymerase III (Pol III) from short interspersed nuclear elements (SINEs), which are high copy number transposable elements in the mouse genome. Unexpectedly have observed significant induction of non-coding RNAs from the B2-SINE subclass of repeat element RNAs in DRG neurons following sciatic nerve injury. We next sought to identify intracellular targets of GI-SINEs, by protein pull-down from sciatic nerve axoplasm using biotinylated B2-SINE RNA as bait. We generated two constructs from the B2 consensus sequence, comprising 5’ (1-75nt) and 3’ (75-166nt) sequences, both predicted to retain their respective structures, and used the constructs to pull down axoplasm proteins for mass spectrometry analysis
project.sampleProcessingProtocol: Protein digestion was performed on-beads. Sample-incubated streptavidin magnetic beads were treated with 10 mM TCEP in 20 mM TEAB, and alkylation followed with 15 mM iodoacetamide, followed by overnight treatment with trypsin, peptide recovery and desalting using a C18 Zip tip for TMTpro 16X labelling and mass spectrometry analysis
project.dataProcessingProtocol: Data were searched against the mouse subsets of the SwissProt database (SwissProt.2019.07.31) (plus the corresponding randomized sequences to calculate FRD on the searches), using Protein Prospector. 1% FDR was allowed.

FactorValue[Bait]: biotinylated B2_5’ (nt 1-75 of the B2-SINE consensus sequence)
Comment[Separation]: 2 μm, 75μm ID x 50 cm PepMap RSLC C18 EasySpray column (Thermo Scientific)
Comment[FragmentationMethod]: Precursor ions were fragmented by HCD using stepped relative collision energies of 30, 35 and 45
Comment[CollisionEnergy]: stepped relative collision energies of 30, 35 and 45
Comment[PrecursorMassTolerance]: 4 ppm in MS (with systematic error correction of 0.5 ppm)
Comment[NumberOfMissedCleavages]: up to two missed cleavages per peptide were allowed.
